reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_192933_bf16_gemm_v1_09758191 at 24.682431 ms is the cleanest compact PTX anchor on the branch so far. It recovered the 201-register, 22,016 B shared-memory surface, restored barrier to 8.14%, and slightly improved runtime beyond the previous accepted 24.689153 ms base. That means the search should spend the pre-checkpoint round on a very narrow, high-information compact-surface tweak instead of another broad branch. The most defensible next move is to remove the final no-refill CTA sync on the penultimate tile: today the loop still executes cp_async_wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads when next_tile exists even if future_tile does not, which means the final handoff pays a barrier even though no stage overwrite happens afterward. I rank a guarded 2-K-stage Pg2s port second because it is still a live latency-hiding idea but materially broader. I keep the wait_sync_collapse family third as a parked fallback only, because two near-base variants already regressed on this surface.
family_audit: accept: the compact 128x128 PTX grouped_rows=4 path at 24.682431 ms is the current accepted base and should remain the active surface.
promote: prefer the smallest high-signal compact-surface barrier trim before reopening any broader async-copy or geometry family.
defer: the wait_sync_collapse family stays open only as a fallback because two recent near-base variants lost on this surface.

directions.name: Skip The Final No-Refill CTA Sync On The Compact PTX Anchor
directions.hypothesis: On the penultimate tile, the current compact PTX loop still pays cp_async_wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads even though future_tile_idx is already out of range and no shared stage will be overwritten. Skipping that final no-refill sync should remove one CTA barrier from the steady-state path without changing registers, stage depth, or the shared-memory budget.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The remaining local tax is unnecessary final-drain barrier overhead on the accepted compact PTX surface.
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate. This is a tiny control-path trim, but it still changes CTA synchronization semantics on the last steady-state handoff.
ranking_notes: This is the highest-signal next move because it isolates one measurable barrier seam on the accepted anchor without reopening any broader family.
The edit is small enough to keep the checkpoint round interpretable even if the gain is modest.
If this does not move the 24.68 ms base, the compact PTX barrier family is likely close to exhausted.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if removing the final sync causes any correctness mismatch or if the change grows into a broader steady-state refactor.
expected_local_changes: Special-case the penultimate-tile handoff so the loop skips the final no-refill __syncthreads.
Keep the compact 128x128 PTX dispatch, grouped_rows=4, and 22,016 B shared-memory footprint unchanged.
Do not combine this trim with any wait_group_1 or refill-before-wait rewrite.

directions.name: Keep The Guarded 2-K-Stage Pg2s Port As The Broader Fallback
directions.hypothesis: The 2-K-stage Pg2s family still targets latency hiding, but it is broader and less interpretable than a one-seam barrier trim. It should stay queued behind the compact final-drain experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: If revisited later, the target remains compact-surface latency hiding and per-tile copy cadence.
directions.risk: High. This family still carries shared-memory and pipeline-structure risk relative to the accepted compact anchor.
ranking_notes: This remains the broader fallback once the compact barrier seam is squeezed further.
It ranks second because the checkpoint round should prefer a smaller, more interpretable experiment first.
If the final-drain trim is flat, this family becomes more attractive again.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the port implies another shared-budget increase or changes the accepted compact dispatch in the same round as another sync tweak.
expected_local_changes: Keep the compact PTX dispatch active.
Only port the 2-K-stage preload and drain structure if the shared-memory budget remains tightly controlled.
Avoid coupling the port with another compact sync-family change.

directions.name: Leave The Wait-Sync-Collapse Family Parked Behind Smaller Compact Tweaks
directions.hypothesis: The wait_sync_collapse family is still conceptually related to the bottleneck, but two recent near-base losses mean it should remain parked until the smaller compact-surface trims are exhausted.
directions.expected_bottleneck: If revisited later, the target would again be the compact loop's wait/refill seam, but not before smaller barrier trims flatten out.
directions.risk: Moderate-high. The family is clearly fragile on the current anchor and no longer deserves another immediate retry.
ranking_notes: This family stays in the tree for completeness, but it no longer deserves immediate selection.
It ranks third because the last two concrete variants both regressed from near-base anchors.
Only revisit it after the smaller compact barrier trims and the guarded 2-K-stage family have been sampled.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if revisiting this family would simply replay the refill-before-wait or wait_group_1 patterns that already lost.
expected_local_changes: Keep the family parked unless smaller compact-surface trims fully flatten out.
Do not replay the refill-before-wait or wait_group_1 variants already measured on this anchor.

notes: Round 15/20 diagnosis emitted from the current accepted compact PTX base; frontier should try the smallest final-drain barrier trim before the checkpoint.
